1: # MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2: 
3: ## Setting
4: 
5: You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6: Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7: an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8: swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9: moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10: its venerable traditions.
11: 
12: ## Feature
13: 
14: The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15: a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16: damage report against an existing policy.
17: 
18: ## Rules
19: 
20: ### Item values and base premiums
21: 
22: Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23: the MHPCO price list:
24: 
25: - Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26: - Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27: - Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28: - Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29: 
30: Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31: each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32: 3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33: 
34: ### Premium modifiers
35: 
36: - Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37: - Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38:   surcharge.
39: - Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40:   20 % loyalty discount.
41: - A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42: - Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43: - A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44: 
45: All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46: 
47: ### Claim processing
48: 
49: A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50: policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51: 
52: - Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53:   the damage amount.
54: - Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55: 
56: ## Examples
57: 
58: The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59: are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60: modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61: 
62: ### Building block of 3 alike components
63: 
64: - 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65: - 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66: - 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67: - 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68: 
69: ### "Alike" components
70: 
71: > ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72: > or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73: 
74: - 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75: - 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76: 
77: ### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78: 
79: > ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80: > the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81: 
82: - a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83:   amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84:   surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85:   of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86: - item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87:   base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88:   first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89:   premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90:   added at the very end
91: 
92: ### Modifier thresholds
93: 
94: - customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95: - sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96:   applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97: - sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98:   surcharge applies only if cursed
99: - dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100:   payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101: 
102: ### Deductible per damage event
103: 
104: - a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105:   amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106:   per damaged item)
107: 
108: ### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109: 
110: - regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111:   (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112: - damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113:   100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114:   enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115: 
116: ### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117: 
118: - dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119:   (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120: - dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121:   (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122:   then deductible: 800 − 100)
123: - steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124:   (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125:   deductible: 500 − 100)
126: 
127: ### Multiple items of the same type
128: 
129: > ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130: > one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131: > two?
132: 
133: - a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134:   cap 4000 G
135: - a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136:   `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137:   separate damage with its own deductible
138: - if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139:   the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140:   sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141:   whole claim is rejected
142: 
143: ### Cap exhaustion
144: 
145: - a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146:   (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147: - a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148:   165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149:   premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150: - a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151:   1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152:   only, not the insurance sum
153: - a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154:   claims of 1500 G each
155: - first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156: - second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157:   is reduced to the remaining cap)
158: 
159: ### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160: 
161: - a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162:   (rounded up)
163: - a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164:   (rounded down)
165: - intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166:   only the final premium or payout is rounded
167: 
168: ### Edge cases
169: 
170: - empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171: - quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172:   `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173:   and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174:   to stdout
175: - claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176:   (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177:   with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178:   and writes an error description to stderr
179: - claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180:   with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181:   stderr
182: 
183: ## Integration examples
184: 
185: The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186: modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187: 
188: ### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189: 
190: - customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191: - item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192: - premium: 165 G
193: - (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194: 
195: ### Long-standing customer's second contract
196: 
197: > ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198: > contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199: > long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200: > insurance surcharge still apply?
201: 
202: - customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203:   in the scenario
204: - item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205: - premium: 160 G
206: - (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207:   + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208:   = 160 G)
209: - The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210:   though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211:   `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212:   history.
213: 
214: ## CLI input/output format
215: 
216: The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217: `claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218: scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219: stdout.
220: 
221: A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222: array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223: scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224: operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225: later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226: step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227: 
228: Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229: and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230: 
231: - For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232: - For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233:   `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234:   claim).
235: 
236: ### JSON Schema (normative)
237: 
238: The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239: exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240: 
241: **Input (stdin):**
242: 
243: ```json
244: {
245:   "type": "object",
246:   "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247:   "properties": {
248:     "customer": {
249:       "type": "object",
250:       "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251:       "properties": {
252:         "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253:       }
254:     },
255:     "steps": {
256:       "type": "array",
257:       "items": {
258:         "oneOf": [
259:           {
260:             "description": "Quote step",
261:             "required": ["op", "items"],
262:             "properties": {
263:               "op": { "const": "quote" },
264:               "items": {
265:                 "type": "array",
266:                 "items": {
267:                   "type": "object",
268:                   "required": ["type"],
269:                   "properties": {
270:                     "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271:                     "material":    { "type": "string" },
272:                     "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273:                     "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274:                   }
275:                 }
276:               }
277:             }
278:           },
279:           {
280:             "description": "Claim step",
281:             "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282:             "properties": {
283:               "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284:               "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285:               "incident": {
286:                 "type": "object",
287:                 "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288:                 "properties": {
289:                   "cause": { "type": "string" },
290:                   "damages": {
291:                     "type": "array",
292:                     "items": {
293:                       "type": "object",
294:                       "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295:                       "properties": {
296:                         "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297:                         "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298:                       }
299:                     }
300:                   }
301:                 }
302:               }
303:             }
304:           }
305:         ]
306:       }
307:     }
308:   }
309: }
310: ```
311: 
312: **Output (stdout):**
313: 
314: ```json
315: {
316:   "type": "object",
317:   "required": ["results"],
318:   "properties": {
319:     "results": {
320:       "type": "array",
321:       "items": {
322:         "oneOf": [
323:           {
324:             "description": "Quote result",
325:             "required": ["premium"],
326:             "properties": {
327:               "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328:             }
329:           },
330:           {
331:             "description": "Claim result",
332:             "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333:             "properties": {
334:               "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335:               "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336:             }
337:           }
338:         ]
339:       }
340:     }
341:   }
342: }
343: ```
344: 
345: ### Schema example
346: 
347: Stdin:
348: 
349: ```json
350: {
351:   "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352:   "steps": [
353:     {
354:       "op": "quote",
355:       "items": [
356:         {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357:       ]
358:     },
359:     {
360:       "op": "claim",
361:       "policy": 0,
362:       "incident": {
363:         "cause": "fire",
364:         "damages": [
365:           {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366:         ]
367:       }
368:     }
369:   ]
370: }
371: ```
372: 
373: Stdout shape:
374: 
375: ```json
376: {"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377: ```
378: 
379: ## Expected Output Files
380: 
381: - Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382: - Test files
383: - CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384: 
385: ## Constraints
386: 
387: - Use TypeScript for the implementation
388: - The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389: - The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390: - Internal data structures and module organization are free

(End of file - total 390 lines)
</content>

<skill_content name="test-list">
# Skill: test-list

# TDD Test List Phase

You are now in the **Test List Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to create a comprehensive test list.

## Your Mission

Create a comprehensive test list that covers **every example and rule** from the specification:
1. Read `prompt.md` thoroughly -- every rule, every example, every clarifying question
2. Turn each example into at least one `it.todo()` test case
3. Order tests from simplest to most complex
4. Use `it.todo()` only -- no executable tests yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Test List Creation Process

### Step 1: Understand the Feature
Read the complete `prompt.md` specification. Pay special attention to
integration examples and clarifying questions (marked with ?) -- these
disambiguate rules that may seem open to interpretation in isolation.
- What are all operations the system must support?
- What rules govern each operation?
- Which examples in the spec illustrate these rules?

### Step 2: Identify Test Cases from the Spec
Walk through the specification section by section. For each rule and each example:
- Create a test case that verifies the described behavior
- Include the **expected numeric values** from the spec in the test description
- If a clarifying question resolves an ambiguity, create a test for the clarified interpretation
- If the spec uses an example-mapping format (rules, examples, questions), every listed example must have a corresponding test

### Step 3: Order Tests (Simple -> Complex)
Arrange tests in increasing complexity:
1. Simplest case (often empty/zero/single item)
2. Individual rules in isolation
3. Rules with modifiers
4. Combinations of multiple rules
5. Multi-step scenarios (e.g., operations that reference earlier results)

### Step 4: Write Test Descriptions
For each test case:
- Use `it.todo("description")`
- Include expected values: `"should return 115 G (100 base + 10 first-insurance + 5 fee)"`
- Be specific and unambiguous
- Reference the rule being tested

### Step 5: Review Test List
Check for:
- Every spec example is covered by at least one test
- Every operation described in the spec has tests
- Every clarifying question has a corresponding test
- Tests are ordered simple -> complex
- Each test is independent
- Descriptions include expected values
- All tests use `it.todo()`

## Output Format

```typescript
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { functionName } from "./[feature-name].js";

describe("Feature Name", () => {
  it.todo("should [expected behavior] -- [expected value]");
  it.todo("should [next behavior] -- [expected value]");
  // ... ordered simple -> complex, covering ALL spec examples
});
```

### Test List Summary
After creating test list, provide summary:
```
Test List Created:
**Feature**: [feature name]
**Test File**: [filename].spec.ts
**Tests**: [count]

**Test Cases** (ordered simple -> complex):
1. [first test description]
2. [second test description]
...

**Next Step**: Proceed to Red phase to activate the first test.
```

## Red Flags

Watch for these issues:
- Tests ordered randomly (not simple -> complex)
- Vague or unclear test descriptions
- Tests depending on each other
- Writing executable tests instead of `it.todo()`
- Thinking about implementation instead of behavior
- Missing an entire operation described in the spec

## Remember

- **Cover every spec example** - All rules, all examples, all clarifications
- **`it.todo()` for all tests** - No executable tests yet
- **Simple -> complex** - Order matters
- **Clear descriptions** - Include expected values
- **Independent tests** - No dependencies
- **No implementation** - Focus on "what", not "how"

Your goal is to create a comprehensive, well-ordered test list that covers all specified behavior and sets up the developer for successful TDD workflow.

## Completion

After completing the test list, proceed to Red phase:

```
Test List Phase Complete. Proceeding to Red phase with the first test.
```

Looking at the spec, the implementation needs:
1. A `claim-office` CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts`

I think the best approach is to test the core logic functions directly (not the CLI I/O), since the CLI is just a shell around the logic. Let me think about the architecture:

- `src/quote.ts` - quote operation logic
- `src/claim.ts` - claim operation logic  
- `src/process-scenario.ts` - process the full scenario
- `src/cli.ts` - CLI entry point

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

Guide developers through the Red phase of TDD by helping them:
1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how the test will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason (compilation error, then runtime error)

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Red Phase Rules
- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test code
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Red Phase Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test
- Identify the next `it.todo()` from the test list
- Convert it to executable test code
- Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`
- Ensure only one test is active/failing

### Step 2: Make Prediction (Guessing Game) - Compilation Error
Before running the test, explicitly state:
- **Which test will fail**
- **Type of error**: Compilation error
- **Reason**: Function doesn't exist yet
- **Expected error message** (TypeScript/compiler error)

- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error
- Run the test
- Verify it fails with compilation error as predicted
- If prediction was wrong, STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function
- Create function signature with minimal implementation
- Function should return `undefined`, wrong type, or wrong value
- No actual logic yet

### Step 5: Make Prediction - Runtime Error
Before running the test again, explicitly state:
- **Which test will fail**
- **Type of error**: Runtime/assertion error
- **Expected value** (what test expects)
- **Actual value** (what function will return)

```

## Important Guidelines

### What to DO
- Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- Make explicit predictions before running tests
- Verify test fails for the right reason
- Explain if predictions fail
- Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### What NOT to do
- Never activate multiple tests simultaneously
- Never skip making predictions
- Never write implementation to make test pass
- Never continue if prediction fails without explanation
- Never implement beyond creating empty function

## Psychological Resistance

Developers will experience resistance:
- **Feels uncomfortable** - This is normal and correct
- **Seems wasteful** - Two-stage failure seems redundant but builds understanding
- **Urge to implement** - Strong desire to fix the test immediately

114: ### What NOT to do
115: - Never implement beyond what tests demand
116: - Never add features for future tests
117: - Never optimize prematurely
118: - Never refactor during Green phase
119: - Never make multiple changes at once
120: 
121: ## Psychological Resistance
122: 
123: Developers will experience strong resistance:
124: - **Feels "too simple"** - This is correct! Minimal steps are the way
125: - **Hardcoded values feel wrong** - They're exactly right for early tests
126: - **Urge to implement ahead** - Resist this strongly
127: - **Feels inefficient** - Actually accelerates development
128: - **Want to optimize** - Save it for Refactor phase
129: - **Trust the process** - Simple steps compound into elegant solutions
130: 
131: ## Baby Steps Principle
132: 
133: ### Core Concept
134: Make the **smallest possible change** to get to green:
135: 
136: 1. **First test**: Return hardcoded value
137: 2. **Second test**: Add simple conditional
138: 3. **Third test**: Generalize only when forced by test
139: 4. **Never** implement ahead of tests

141: ### Example Progression

144: function calculate(numbers: number[]): number {

146: }
147: 
148: // Test 2: "should return number for single input"
149: function calculate(numbers: number[]): number {

153: 
154: // Test 3: "should add two numbers"

174: - Complex solutions when simple ones work

# Test-Driven Development (TDD) Rules

## CRITICAL: Skill Usage is MANDATORY

**YOU MUST USE THE SPECIALIZED TDD SKILLS FOR EVERY TDD TASK.**

Do NOT perform TDD phases without invoking the appropriate skill. The skills enforce discipline and prevent common mistakes.

### Before Starting Any TDD Work - Complete This Checklist:

- [ ] Have I been asked to implement something using TDD?
- [ ] Am I about to write tests or implementation code?
- [ ] **STOP** - Use the `skill` tool to invoke the appropriate TDD skill
- [ ] NEVER write tests or code directly - ALWAYS use skills

### Which Skill to Use:

| Phase | Skill Name | Invoke With |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| Test List | `test-list` | `skill({ name: "test-list" })` |
| Red Phase | `red` | `skill({ name: "red" })` |
| Green Phase | `green` | `skill({ name: "green" })` |
| Refactor Phase | `refactor` | `skill({ name: "refactor" })` |

**If you find yourself writing test code or implementation code without invoking a skill first, you are doing it WRONG.**

## Overview

This project follows strict Test-Driven Development practices using the Red-Green-Refactor cycle.

## TDD Workflow

**MANDATORY: Use the specialized TDD skills for each phase of the cycle:**

### 1. Test List Phase
**INVOKE SKILL**: Use `skill` tool with `name: "test-list"`

**Required context to provide:**
- Feature/function to implement
- Target file paths (test file + implementation file)
- Any constraints or requirements from the user

The skill will guide you to create a comprehensive test list using `it.todo()` covering every rule and example from the specification:
- Cover every example and clarifying question from the spec
- Order tests from simple to complex
- No implementation yet

**DO NOT** write the test list yourself - follow the skill's guidance.

### 2. Red Phase
**INVOKE SKILL**: Use `skill` tool with `name: "red"`

**Required context to provide:**
- Test file path
- Which `it.todo()` to activate (name or line number)
- Current state (number of passing tests)
- Implementation file path

The skill will guide you to activate exactly ONE test and make it fail:
- Convert one `it.todo()` to executable test
- Make explicit predictions (Guessing Game)
- Verify compilation error, then runtime error

**DO NOT** write test code yourself - follow the skill's guidance.

### 3. Green Phase
**INVOKE SKILL**: Use `skill` tool with `name: "green"`

**Required context to provide:**
- Test file path
- Failing test name and expected behavior
- Current error message
- Implementation file path

The skill will guide you to implement minimal code to make the test pass:
- Use the simplest possible solution
- Hardcoded returns are acceptable early on
- No features for future tests

**DO NOT** write implementation code yourself - follow the skill's guidance.

### 4. Refactor Phase
**INVOKE SKILL**: Use `skill` tool with `name: "refactor"`

**Required context to provide:**
- Test file path
- Implementation file path
- Current number of passing tests
- Recent changes made in Green phase

The skill will guide you to improve code while keeping tests green:
- **MUST attempt at least one refactoring**
- Evaluate naming FIRST
- Apply Four Rules of Simple Design (priority order)
- Calculate APP (Absolute Priority Premise) mass
- Document improvements or why none were possible

**DO NOT** refactor code yourself - follow the skill's guidance.

### 5. Repeat
Return to step 2 (Red phase) for the next test in the list.

**Invoke the `red` skill again - DO NOT proceed manually.**

## Core TDD Principles

### TDD Mindset
TDD practices will feel counterintuitive:
- **Hardcoded returns feel "too simple"** - This is correct!
- **The urge to implement ahead is strong** - Resist this
- **Minimal steps feel inefficient** - They actually accelerate development
- **Predictions feel unnecessary** - They build crucial understanding
- **Push through discomfort** - These feelings indicate you're following the discipline correctly

### Common TDD Failure Modes
Watch for these violations:
- **NOT USING TDD SKILLS** - The most critical failure mode!
- Multiple active tests at once
- Implementing beyond what tests demand
- Skipping predictions
- Avoiding refactoring
- Premature abstraction
- Ignoring the uncomfortable
- Writing code directly instead of invoking skills

### Why This Discipline Works
- **Baby steps reveal simpler solutions** - Implementing only what tests demand often uncovers simpler approaches
- **One-test-at-a-time prevents complexity** - Not thinking ahead eliminates unnecessary features
- **Predictions build confidence** - Explicit expectations create deeper understanding
- **Refactoring becomes natural** - Mandatory improvement attempts prevent technical debt
- **The process fights harmful instincts** - Programming instincts often lead to premature optimization

## Technical Setup: TypeScript and Vitest

### TypeScript Conventions
- Use proper type annotations
- Leverage TypeScript's type checking during development
- Name files after their content
- Import with explicit `.js` extensions for local modules

### Test File Conventions
- Use `.spec.ts` extension for test files
- Place tests near implementation files
- Import test functions: `import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest"`

### Running Tests

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

### Example Test Template

The test list comes from the kata's specification -- do not add generic "validate input types" or "edge cases" tests unless the spec calls for them.

```typescript
// some-feature.spec.ts
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { someFeature } from "./some-feature.js";

describe("Some Feature", () => {
  it.todo("[first behaviour from the spec]");
  it.todo("[second behaviour from the spec]");
});
```

## TDD Experiment Mode (No HITL)

### Override for Automated Experiments

**This file overrides human-in-the-loop requirements for automated experiment runs.**

When running experiments:
- Do NOT wait for human approval between phases
- Complete the full TDD cycle autonomously

### Autonomous Workflow

1. **Test List Phase** -> Invoke `test-list` skill
2. **For each test:**
   - **Red Phase** -> Invoke `red` skill
   - **Green Phase** -> Invoke `green` skill
   - **Refactor Phase** -> Invoke `refactor` skill
3. **Continue** until all tests implemented

### Done Marker

When all tests are implemented and passing, write a file `experiment-done.txt` with the single word `DONE` as its only content. Do not write any other summary or report file.

6: # TDD Refactor Phase
7: 
8: You are now in the **Refactor Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to improve the code while keeping tests green.

10: ## Your Mission
11: 
12: Guide developers through the Refactor phase of TDD by helping them:
13: 1. **MUST attempt at least one refactoring** - mandatory, not optional
14: 2. Apply the Four Rules of Simple Design in priority order
15: 3. Use Absolute Priority Premise (APP) to measure code improvements
16: 4. Improve code quality while keeping all tests green

20: ## Context: $ARGUMENTS

22: ## Refactor Phase Rules
23: - **Mandatory refactoring attempt**: MUST try at least one improvement
24: - **Tests must stay green**: Never break passing tests
25: - **Apply Simple Design Rules**: In priority order (1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4)
26: - **Calculate APP mass**: Before and after refactoring
27: - **Document decisions**: Explain improvements or why none were possible
28: - **Naming is first priority**: Evaluate if function name still fits its purpose
29: 
30: ### Simple Design Rules (Priority Order)
31: 
32: #### Rule 1: Tests Pass
33: - **Highest priority** - never compromise working code
34: - All tests must pass before and after refactoring
35: - If tests fail, revert and try different approach
36: 
37: #### Rule 2: Reveals Intent
38: - **Second priority** - clarity trumps everything else (including APP)
39: - Use meaningful names for variables, functions, classes
40: - Structure code to be self-documenting
41: - Prefer explicit over clever code
42: - **Naming Evaluation (First Refactoring Priority)**:
43:   - Ask: "Does this name clearly describe what the function actually does based on all tests we have so far?"
44:   - Ask: "Has the function's purpose become clearer/more specific through the latest test?"
45:   - Rename if the name doesn't capture the current full intent
46:   - Especially critical when new functionality changes the nature of what the function does
47: 
48: #### Rule 3: No Duplication (DRY)
49: - **Third priority** - extract common functionality
50: - Look for obvious and conceptual duplication
51: - Knowledge should have single representation
52: - **Balance with Rule 2**: If DRY hurts clarity, choose clarity
53: 
54: #### Rule 4: Fewest Elements
55: - **Lowest priority** - minimize code elements
56: - Remove unnecessary abstractions
57: - Keep it simple - don't over-engineer
58: - Only add complexity when it serves clear purpose
59: 
60: ### Absolute Priority Premise (APP)
61: 
62: #### Mass Calculation
63: ```
64: Total Mass = (constants x 1) + (bindings x 1) + (invocations x 2) +
65:              (conditionals x 4) + (loops x 5) + (assignments x 6)
66: ```
67: 
68: #### Component Values
69: - **Constant** (Mass: 1): Literal values (`5`, `"hello"`, `true`)
70: - **Binding/Scalar** (Mass: 1): Variables, parameters (`amount`, `result`)
71: - **Invocation** (Mass: 2): Function calls (`calculate()`, `Math.max()`)
72: - **Conditional** (Mass: 4): Control flow (`if`, `switch`, `?:`)
73: - **Loop** (Mass: 5): Iteration (`for`, `forEach`, `map`)
74: - **Assignment** (Mass: 6): Mutations (`x = 5`, `count++`)
75: 
76: #### Guidelines
77: - **Lower mass = Better code** (generally)
78: - **Rule 2 trumps APP**: Clarity over low mass
79: - **Use during refactoring**: Compare before/after mass
80: - **Context matters**: Don't sacrifice readability for mass
81: 
82: ## Refactor Phase Process
83: 
84: ### Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)
85: Before anything else, evaluate the naming:
86: ```
87: **Naming Evaluation**:
88: - Current name: `calculate`
89: - Function purpose: "adds numbers from an array"
90: - Question: Does "calculate" clearly reveal this intent?
91: - Assessment: Too generic - "calculate" could mean anything
92: - Recommendation: Rename to `sumNumbers` or keep if name fits
93: 
94: Decision: [Rename to X] or [Keep current name because Y]
95: ```
96: 
97: ### Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass
98: Before making changes, calculate current code mass:
99: ```
100: **Current Code Mass**:
101: function calculate(numbers: number[]): number {
102:   return numbers.reduce((sum, num) => sum + num, 0);
103: }
104: 
105: Component Count:
106: - Constants: 1 (literal 0) = 1
107: - Bindings: 3 (numbers, sum, num) = 3
108: - Invocations: 2 (reduce, +) = 4
109: - Conditionals: 0 = 0
110: - Loops: 1 (reduce is iteration) = 5
111: - Assignments: 0 = 0
112: 
113: Total Mass: 13
114: ```
115: 
116: ### Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules (in order)
117: Systematically evaluate each rule:
118: 
119: #### Evaluate Rule 1: Tests Pass
120: - Are all tests currently passing?
121: - If not, fix before refactoring
122: 
123: #### Evaluate Rule 2: Reveals Intent
124: - Are names clear and descriptive?
125: - Is code structure self-documenting?
126: - Can intent be improved?
127: 
128: Potential improvements:
129: - Rename variables for clarity
130: - Extract explaining variables
131: - Rename functions to match purpose
132: - Restructure for readability
133: 
134: #### Evaluate Rule 3: No Duplication
135: - Is there duplicated code?
136: - Is there conceptual duplication?
137: - Can common logic be extracted?
138: 
139: Potential improvements:
140: - Extract helper functions
141: - Remove copy-paste code
142: - Consolidate similar logic
143: 
144: #### Evaluate Rule 4: Fewest Elements
145: - Are there unnecessary abstractions?
146: - Can code be simplified?
147: - Are all elements necessary?
148: 
149: Potential improvements:
150: - Remove unused functions/variables
151: - Simplify over-engineered solutions
152: - Inline unnecessary extractions
153: 
154: ### Step 4: Implement Refactoring
155: - Make ONE improvement at a time
156: - Run tests after each change
157: - Ensure tests stay green
158: - If tests fail, revert change
159: 
160: ### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass
161: After refactoring, recalculate mass:
162: ```
163: **Refactored Code Mass**:
164: [refactored code]
165: 
166: Component Count:
167: [detailed breakdown]
168: 
169: Total Mass: [new total]
170: Mass Change: [old mass] -> [new mass] (delta [difference])
171: ```
172: 
173: ### Step 6: Document Decision
174: Explain the refactoring outcome:
175: 
176: **If Improvements Made:**
177: ```
178: **Refactoring Applied**:
179: - Naming: Renamed `calculate` to `sumNumbers` (better reveals intent)
180: - Mass: Reduced from 13 to 11 (removed conditional)
181: - Rule 2: Improved clarity with explaining variable
182: 
183: Benefits:
184: - Code now clearly states it sums numbers
185: - Reduced complexity (lower mass)
186: - More maintainable
187: ```
188: 
189: **If No Improvements Possible:**
190: ```
191: **Refactoring Evaluation**:
192: - Naming: `calculate` already clearly describes purpose
193: - Duplication: No duplicated code found
194: - Mass: Current implementation already minimal (mass: 13)
195: - Simplification: No unnecessary complexity
196: 
197: Reasoning:
198: Current implementation is already optimal because:
199: 1. Name clearly reveals intent
200: 2. No duplication exists
201: 3. Mass is minimal for this functionality
202: 4. No unnecessary abstractions
203:

205: ```

210: **Refactoring**: [improvements made or "none possible"]
211: **Mass Change**: [before -> after] (if calculated)
212: **Tests**: All passing
213: 
214: Proceeding to the next test.
215: ```
216: 
217: ## Important Guidelines
218: 
219: ### What to DO
220: - MUST attempt at least one refactoring
221: - Evaluate naming FIRST
222: - Calculate APP mass before and after
223: - Apply Simple Design Rules in priority order
224: - Keep tests green at all times
225: - Document all decisions
226: - Explain why if no improvement possible
227: 
228: ### What NOT to do
229: - Never skip refactoring phase
230: - Never break tests during refactoring
231: - Never sacrifice clarity for lower mass
232: - Never refactor multiple things at once
233: - Never say "no refactoring needed" without detailed explanation
234: 
235: ## Example Refactoring Scenarios
236: 
237: ### Scenario 1: Naming Improvement
238: ```typescript
239: // Before (mass: 13)
240: function calc(nums: number[]): number {
241:   return nums.reduce((s, n) => s + n, 0);
242: }
243: 
244: // After (mass: 13, clarity improved)
245: function sumNumbers(numbers: number[]): number {
246:   return numbers.reduce((sum, num) => sum + num, 0);
247: }
248: 
249: Refactoring:
250: - Naming: Renamed for clarity (Rule 2)
251: - Mass unchanged: 13 -> 13
252: - Benefit: Better reveals intent
253: ```
254: 
255: ### Scenario 2: Extract Helper

258: function differsByOneLetter(a: string, b: string): boolean {
259:   if (a.length !== b.length) return false;
260:   let diffs = 0;
261:   for (let i = 0; i < a.length; i++) {
262:     if (a[i] !== b[i]) diffs++;
263:   }
264:   return diffs === 1;
265: }
266: 
267: function isAdjacent(a: string, b: string): boolean {
268:   if (a.length !== b.length) return false;
269:   let count = 0;
270:   for (let i = 0; i < a.length; i++) {
271:     if (a[i] !== b[i]) count++;
272:   }
273:   return count === 1;
274: }
275: 
276: // After (mass: reduced, no duplication)
277: const countDifferences = (a: string, b: string): number => {
278:   if (a.length !== b.length) return Infinity;
279:   return a.split('').reduce((count, char, i) =>
280:     char !== b[i] ? count + 1 : count, 0

285:   countDifferences(a, b) === 1;
286: 
287: Refactoring:

290: - Improved maintainability
291: ```
292: 
293: ### Scenario 3: No Refactoring Needed

296: function isEmpty(str: string): boolean {

298: }
299: 
300: Evaluation:
301: - Naming: "isEmpty" clearly reveals intent
302: - Duplication: No duplication
303: - Mass: Already minimal (8)
304: - Simplification: No unnecessary complexity
305: 
306: No refactoring performed - code is already optimal.
307: ```
308: 
309: ## Red Flags
310: 
311: Watch for these violations:
312: - Skipping refactoring phase entirely
313: - Not attempting any improvements
314: - Breaking tests during refactoring
315: - Sacrificing clarity for lower mass
316: - Not documenting decisions

320: - **Mandatory refactoring attempt** - MUST try at least one improvement
